narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 35, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from early and later years showing you were in the lower end of your class—2nd decile in Year 1 and 1st decile in Year 3, compared to typical students who average around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 61.6% probability that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This prediction wasn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment drawing from thousands of similar student outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, as being in the bottom decile signaled to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers, which strongly correlated with bar struggles in past cases. Next, your LSAT score played a key role, but in a positive way—it pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, since 35 is close to the average of 36.7 and showed solid reasoning ability despite not being exceptional. Your undergraduate GPA also helped your case, nudging things toward success because your 2.90, while below the typical 3.21, still indicated decent academic foundation work. Overall, your lower Year 3 decile outweighed these positives, setting you apart from typical admitted students who maintain mid-range class rankings throughout. Family income and full-time status had less sway here, as middle income is common and full-time is the norm for over 90% of applicants. This rejection stings, especially with your determination as a Hispanic man breaking into law, but it's not the end—many in similar positions rebound strongly. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening later-year grades through targeted prep or additional coursework, and seeking bar passage mentorship programs tailored for underrepresented groups like yours. Reapplying after addressing these could flip the model's view, and schools often value persistence in applicants from diverse backgrounds like Hispanic males. Keep pushing; your story has real potential.
